dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a sick person reported with an accidental overdose on blood pressure medications.
Hi, I'm Alex with EMS. What made you decide to call 911 today?
I accidentally took my 8pm dose of blood pressure medication early-amlodipine and carvedilol-and now I feel weak.
Can you tell me about the moment the weakness started and if you experienced anything else?
I took both pills right away shortly before calling 911. I don't have chest pain, shortness of breath, or any pain, just weakness and anxiety.
Eye opening is 4, verbal response is 5, and motor response is 6. You're fully alert and oriented.
No signs of unilateral weakness or motor deficits are present.
Pulse is 72 and regular.
Respirations are 18 and unlabored.
Blood pressure is 145/65.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
Glucose levels are displaying as zero, but there are no symptoms consistent with hypoglycemia.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with regular readings on 4-lead monitoring.
We obtained a 12-lead EKG, which showed sinus rhythm with minimal evidence of left ventricular hypertrophy due to high QRS voltages.
You're fully oriented: eye-4, verbal-5, motor-6, with clear speech and no slurring.
You are ambulatory with a steady gait and can walk using your walker or one person at your side.
We placed you in a position of comfort on the stretcher.
You take aspirin, buspirone, lisinopril/HCTZ, amlodipine, and carvedilol daily. Your medical history includes hypertension, shoulder repair surgery, and a TAH.
You're allergic to penicillin and aspirin, which "make your heart race." You're also allergic to chocolate.
You have no complaints of bowel pain or changes.
You say your caregiver is providing adequate care at home and you feel safe. You stated they're not related and have a "weird" name that you can't recall.
As per your wishes, you will be transported to St. Mary's, your preferred hospital.
During transport, we rechecked your vitals. Blood pressure is now 124/70, and pulse is 64. EKG remains in normal sinus rhythm.
A second 12-lead EKG showed no changes: normal findings for a 90-year-old female with sinus rhythm.
We are following medical - overdose/poisoning - opioid (Protocol 7-1).
Due to ED overcrowding, we used a 2-person sheet drag to transfer you from stretcher to bed.
Turnover report includes your vitals, medications, allergy history, and updates on various caregiver concerns.
From initial contact through ED arrival, there were no new symptoms.
You have no bowel complaints or changes in bowel movement.
The caregiver mentioned concerns about the patient's will and safety at home. She expressed worries about potential conflict.
No adventitious breath sounds, no asymmetry of chest movements. Work of breathing is normal.
No shortness of breath reported.
Your pulse is regular, not thready or bounding.
No lymphadenopathy or neck masses observed. No nuchal rigidity.
No rashes or bite marks. Skin color is typical with no jaundice.
Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
No evidence of extra-ocular movement deficits or visual abnormalities.
No drainage, obstruction, or foreign bodies in the ears, nose, or throat.
Swallowing is normal with no reported difficulties.
Breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Heart sounds are regular with no arrhythmias detected.
Peripheral pulses are strong and equal.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender. Bowel sounds present and normal.
All extremities move normally. No deformities noted.
Rechecked your pulse, respiration, and blood pressure-no changes from the previous assessment.
EKG remains stable in sinus rhythm.
Respirations remain at 18 and unlabored.
Pulse is 64 and regular.
Blood pressure is 124/70.
Oxygen saturation still at 99% on room air.
Glucose levels still read zero but no hypoglycemic symptoms.
Noted no new symptoms or changes in your condition during the trip.
You continue to report no changes in symptoms or overall condition.
No new or worsening pain since our initial evaluation.
No chest pain reported during rechecks.
Still no shortness of breath or other issues.
You remain fully oriented and engaged during the entire transport.
EKG has remained stable and shows no acute changes.
You were slid from stretcher to bed using a 2-person sheet drag.
All documentation, vitals, and care details have been passed to the receiving ED team.
You are now assigned a bed and being slid securely into position.
No abnormal symptoms observed or reported at destination.
All patient belongings have been accounted for and handed over with documentation.
No medications or additional treatments were administered during transport.
No improvement or decline noted during contact with the ED.
Patient was made comfortable and maintained position of safety.
No significant difficulty with patient mobility or assistance.
Absence of dizziness, or other pertinent symptoms.
Patient had no exposure to environmental toxin or injury.
No adverse allergic reaction noted post-assistance.
Smooth handover between EMS and ED staff including all documentation.
No delays or interruptions during transport to the ED.
No medication, IV, or procedural errors reported.
All equipment and controls were available and functioning during transport.
No variation in patient's normal behavior or condition during trip.
Re-assessment of all systems confirmed no new findings.
Patient is being processed and stabilized in emergency department.
Reconfirmed no chest pain, shortness of breath, or worsening anxiety.
Post-arrival, patient remains in normal state and condition.
No medications administered were transferred with patient.
No signs of patient discomfort or increased anxiety during arrival.
Handover of all care notes, vitals, and verbal report complete.
EPCR is complete and sent to relevant databases for filing.
No unstated or unrecorded patient events found.
Final vitals remain: BP 124/70, pulse 64, RR 18, SpO2 99%.
Final 12-lead EKG performed and recorded for ED review.
Patient confirms living in a safe environment despite caregiver's worries.
Background on face-to-face caregiver conflict shared with ED team.
Patient affirmed consent for transport and medical care provided.
No evidence of seizure activity during transport.
No signs of stroke in terms of face, speech, or arm asymmetry.
Transport has concluded with successful arrival at designated hospital.
All medical notes and data surrounding this transport have been reported.
Patient care and transport complete as per protocol 7-1.
